name: Bus Passengers

metadata.tagline: The brightening, the lift.

sections.About: The brightening, the lift.
sections.Context: Something is going well. Right now, in this configuration. It might not last; it doesn't need to.
sections.Lineage: Pixar Inside Out (Ekman / Keltner). Public-domain emotion archetype.
sections.Mystery: Joy is older than language. Other animals know it. You are participating in something far larger than your own pleasure when you feel it — and the not-knowing of that is part of its sweetness.
sections.Sensing: An expansion in the chest. Lightness behind the eyes. A lifting at the mouth that came before the smile.
sections.Thinking: Pattern-matching toward what's working. Building case for sustaining the moment. Often unconsciously bargaining: if I notice it, will it leave?
sections.Thoughts: This is good. I want more. Look how good this is.
sections.Perception: Filters toward the warm, the light, the people who are with you. Often dims the edges of the room.
sections.Where this isn't enough: When joy starts feeling like something to grip or perform, that's the cue to put the phone down and let it be without commentary.

metadata.tagline: The honest weight.

sections.About: The honest weight.
sections.Context: Something has actually been lost or is being lost. Something real that mattered. The grief is not a malfunction.
sections.Lineage: Pixar Inside Out (Ekman / Keltner). Public-domain emotion archetype.
sections.Mystery: Sadness is one of the oldest songs. Many traditions consider it sacred. The body knows how to grieve in ways the mind has never been taught.
sections.Sensing: A pulling-down in the chest. A heaviness behind the eyes. The body wanting to fold inward, not outward.
sections.Thinking: Cataloguing what's missing. Tracing the shape of the loss. Often resisting the catalogue while making it.
sections.Thoughts: This is hard. I don't have what I need. Something is gone.
sections.Perception: Filters toward absence. The room may feel emptier than it is. Voices may sound farther away.
sections.Where this isn't enough: If the heaviness is constant and the days are flat, this scaffolding stops being enough. A therapist, a doctor, or a trusted friend is the right next step.

metadata.tagline: The boundary-feeling.

sections.About: The boundary-feeling.
sections.Context: A boundary has been crossed, or a value violated. Or a value is being defended that you were not allowed to defend earlier in life.
sections.Lineage: Pixar Inside Out (Ekman / Keltner). Public-domain emotion archetype.
sections.Mystery: Anger as a teacher of boundaries is older than the courts. In some traditions it is a form of love refusing to lie.
sections.Sensing: Heat in the chest, jaw, hands. Pressure behind the eyes. The body recruiting itself for something.
sections.Thinking: Building case for action. Often confusing the trigger with the cause. Frequently arrives after a slower hurt that wasn't named in time.
sections.Thoughts: This is wrong. This shouldn't be happening. Someone should answer for this.
sections.Perception: Narrows. Tunnel-vision toward the perceived violator. Filters out context and counter-evidence.
sections.Where this isn't enough: If the heat keeps redirecting at the same person and you cannot find what's actually under it, an outside witness — therapist, mediator, trusted friend — helps more than another round of journaling.

metadata.tagline: The sharper edge of attention.

sections.About: The sharper edge of attention.
sections.Context: Something might be at stake. Sometimes the stakes are real and present. Sometimes they were real twenty years ago and the body has not been told the news.
sections.Lineage: Pixar Inside Out (Ekman / Keltner). Public-domain emotion archetype.
sections.Mystery: Fear is a gift older than language. It has saved every ancestor who passed life on to you. The fact that you are alive is partly because fear was paying attention.
sections.Sensing: A held breath. A coolness in the hands. The skin alert in places it usually isn't.
sections.Thinking: Running rehearsals of bad outcomes. Building case for vigilance. Often confusing imagined threat with present threat.
sections.Thoughts: Something is wrong. I need to be ready. I cannot let my guard down.
sections.Perception: Narrows toward perceived danger. Edges of the room go sharper; safe things become invisible.
sections.Where this isn't enough: Panic that arrives without a stimulus, or fear that organizes most of the day, is past the line where reflection is the right tool. Clinical support is.

metadata.tagline: The instinctive no.

sections.About: The instinctive no.
sections.Context: Something violates a value, taste, or boundary that lives in this body. The disgust may be teaching you something about yourself, or about an inheritance.
sections.Lineage: Pixar Inside Out (Ekman / Keltner). Public-domain emotion archetype.
sections.Mystery: The instinct that lets a body know what to push away is one of the oldest immune systems. It is not always wise; it is always old.
sections.Sensing: A pulling-back in the throat or mouth. A skin crawl. The body recoiling slightly before the mind has decided.
sections.Thinking: Sorting in and out. Often inherited from family, culture, or trauma rather than freshly chosen. Sometimes accurate, sometimes not.
sections.Thoughts: This is wrong for me. This contaminates something.
sections.Perception: Filters toward the offending object or person. Other things in the field can disappear.
sections.Where this isn't enough: When disgust attaches to whole categories of people, it has stopped serving you and started repeating something you didn't choose. Therapy and reading outside your tradition are better than journaling.

metadata.tagline: The forward-leaning rehearsal.

sections.About: The forward-leaning rehearsal.
sections.Context: Something is uncertain. Sometimes the uncertainty is real and active. Sometimes the body is protecting you from a different uncertainty that already happened.
sections.Lineage: Pixar Inside Out 2 (Damour / Keltner). Public-domain emotion archetype.
sections.Mystery: Anxiety is not a malfunction of a calm mind. It is the mind doing what minds were made to do, applied to a situation older minds did not face. The body's care is older than the worry.
sections.Sensing: A tightness behind the breastbone. Restlessness in the hands or legs. The body acting as if it must move soon, even when it doesn't.
sections.Thinking: Generating contingencies. Building forecasts. Often mistaking the rehearsal for the protection.
sections.Thoughts: What if. What about. I should plan for. I should be ready.
sections.Perception: Filters toward future scenarios. The present can become hard to land in.
sections.Where this isn't enough: If anxiety is the climate, not the weather — if every day arrives this way — this is not the right tool. A clinician is.

metadata.tagline: The map of what you want.

sections.About: The map of what you want.
sections.Context: Something you want is showing up clearly in someone else's life. The clarity is the gift, even when the feeling is corrosive.
sections.Lineage: Pixar Inside Out 2 (Damour / Keltner). Public-domain emotion archetype.
sections.Mystery: Envy reveals the contour of a longing you may not have admitted to yourself. The longing is the data. The other person is the messenger, not the cause.
sections.Sensing: A tightness in the gut or chest. A heat in the face. Sometimes a nausea.
sections.Thinking: Comparison engines running in the background. Often using the other person as a map of the self's longing while pretending to be about them.
sections.Thoughts: They have what I should have. Why not me. I deserved that more.
sections.Perception: Filters toward the other person's life. Often misses what they don't have or what it cost them.
sections.Where this isn't enough: When envy turns into action that diminishes the other, the practice has crossed a line. Stepping back, talking to a friend or therapist, and asking what the longing actually is — that's the work.

metadata.tagline: The visible self, briefly aware of itself.

sections.About: The visible self, briefly aware of itself.
sections.Context: You did something visible that crossed a social line. Sometimes the line was real; sometimes it was inherited and rigid.
sections.Lineage: Pixar Inside Out 2 (Damour / Keltner). Public-domain emotion archetype.
sections.Mystery: Belonging matters because you are an animal made of relationship. Embarrassment is a cousin of belonging. It is not weakness.
sections.Sensing: Heat in the face. A wanting-to-fold-up. A breath that didn't come.
sections.Thinking: Imagining yourself from outside. Building case that you have failed a social standard. Often catastrophizing the visibility.
sections.Thoughts: They saw. I look bad. I want to disappear.
sections.Perception: Filters toward your own image in others' eyes. Filters out their actual likely indifference.
sections.Where this isn't enough: If embarrassment becomes shame and shame becomes hiding, the work is past this scaffold. Friends who love you. Therapy. The right kind of small repair, in person, when you are ready.

metadata.tagline: The flatness with no name.

sections.About: The flatness with no name.
sections.Context: Something is being held at a distance. Sometimes you are tired in a real way that needs rest. Sometimes you are protecting yourself from something specific that's underneath.
sections.Lineage: Pixar Inside Out 2 (Damour / Keltner). Public-domain emotion archetype.
sections.Mystery: The flatness is not nothing. It is something covering something else. The body is waiting for you to be ready.
sections.Sensing: A heaviness or a numbness, depending on the day. The body present but quiet.
sections.Thinking: Disengaged scanning. Often a defense against feeling something more specific that the body is not yet ready to feel.
sections.Thoughts: Nothing matters. Whatever. I'm bored. I should be doing something.
sections.Perception: Everything slightly grey. Nothing pulls attention. Things that would normally interest you don't.
sections.Where this isn't enough: Persistent flatness with low energy and no joy in things that used to give joy is past this tool's range. A doctor and a therapist together is the right next step.

metadata.tagline: The one who tries to keep everything from breaking.

sections.About: The one who tries to keep everything from breaking.
sections.Context: There are real responsibilities and the world is genuinely complicated. The manager is not wrong; it is just very tired.
sections.Lineage: Generic parts-work language inspired by IFS (Schwartz). 'Internal Family Systems' is trademarked; this card uses common-language parts framing only.
sections.Mystery: This passenger is protecting parts of you that learned, long ago, that being managed kept everyone safe. It is a form of love that forgot it could rest.
sections.Sensing: Tension in the shoulders or jaw. A slight forward-leaning. Often disconnected from breath.
sections.Thinking: Strategic, anticipatory, controlling. Maintaining the daily structures. Often working without rest.
sections.Thoughts: I have to think about this. I have to plan. I have to keep us safe.
sections.Perception: Hyper-aware of risks, schedules, expectations. Less aware of present pleasure or rest.
sections.Where this isn't enough: If the manager runs all night, the work is not in this tool. Sleep, somatic therapy, a long break, or someone else taking the wheel for a while.

metadata.tagline: The one who breaks the glass.

sections.About: The one who breaks the glass.
sections.Context: Something more uncomfortable is right behind this. The firefighter is not stupid; it is doing exactly what it was hired to do.
sections.Lineage: Generic parts-work language inspired by IFS (Schwartz). 'Internal Family Systems' is trademarked; this card uses common-language parts framing only.
sections.Mystery: This passenger is keeping the system running. If you forced it out without anything to replace it, the underlying part it protects would flood. Respect it.
sections.Sensing: A grabbing-quality in the body. Hands that already know what they want to do.
sections.Thinking: Reaching for the immediate intervention — food, drink, scroll, work, drama. Often arrives when something painful gets too close.
sections.Thoughts: I need it now. Just this one thing. It will help.
sections.Perception: Tunnel toward the relief. The thing that was hurting drops out of view temporarily.
sections.Where this isn't enough: When the firefighter's intervention is itself causing harm — to your body, your relationships, your work — this scaffolding cannot replace community, therapy, or specific recovery support.

metadata.tagline: The young one carrying what nobody could hold then.

sections.About: The young one carrying what nobody could hold then.
sections.Context: Something happened, often early, that nobody at the time could metabolize with you. The exile is carrying it on behalf of the system.
sections.Lineage: Generic parts-work language inspired by IFS (Schwartz). The cautionary scope of this card is intentional.
sections.Mystery: This passenger is the part of you that kept feeling when the rest of you had to keep going. The fact that you can sit with it now means something has changed in your capacity to be a witness.
sections.Sensing: A specific quality of holding in the chest or belly. Sometimes a child's posture in an adult body.
sections.Thinking: Often pre-verbal. The patterns are old, formed before language was the main currency.
sections.Thoughts: Sometimes there are no thoughts here, just feelings. When there are thoughts: 'It was my fault. I was alone. Nobody came.'
sections.Perception: Filters toward signs of the original abandonment, even decades later.
sections.Where this isn't enough: Direct work with exiled parts is not for solo journaling. A therapist trained in parts work, somatic experiencing, or a similar approach is the right container.

metadata.tagline: The one who can be with all of it.

sections.About: The one who can be with all of it.
sections.Context: This is the part of you that has always been here, even when it felt unreachable. Not above the others; alongside them, with more room.
sections.Lineage: Generic parts-work language for the centering capacity (related to IFS 'Self', Voice Dialogue 'Aware Ego', and many contemplative traditions' witness/observer).
sections.Mystery: Many traditions have a name for this — Buddha-nature, the witness, the deep self, the soul. Each tradition is pointing at something hard to point at.
sections.Sensing: A settling. Breath returns. Shoulders drop. The body remembers it is not alone.
sections.Thinking: Spacious, unhurried, curious. Less about strategy than about presence.
sections.Thoughts: Few thoughts. Mostly: 'I see you.' 'You are welcome here.' 'I am not going anywhere.'
sections.Perception: Wider. Holds multiple passengers at once without choosing sides.
sections.Where this isn't enough: Self-energy is the resource, not the answer. When the system is in crisis, finding this is the first step, not the only one.

metadata.tagline: The one who learned that pre-emptive scolding kept you safer.

sections.About: The one who learned that pre-emptive scolding kept you safer.
sections.Context: At some point, scolding yourself before someone else could was the strategy that kept you in good standing. It worked. It is no longer the only available strategy.
sections.Lineage: Common across parts-work traditions (IFS, Voice Dialogue, Gestalt). Generic framing.
sections.Mystery: The voice you hear is often not your own. It is a recording of someone who was once in your environment. You are not the recording. You are the one listening.
sections.Sensing: A clenching in the chest. A specific kind of held breath. Sometimes a small audible 'hmph' that no one else hears.
sections.Thinking: Comparison engines, perfectionist standards, often quoting verbatim from someone who scolded you long ago.
sections.Thoughts: Not good enough. They're going to see. You should know better. You're a fraud.
sections.Perception: Filters toward your own flaws and others' implicit judgments. Filters out evidence of your competence.
sections.Where this isn't enough: When the critic dominates the day and erodes basic functioning, structured therapy is the work. This card is for noticing, not for replacing the work.

metadata.tagline: The one who keeps you moving.

sections.About: The one who keeps you moving.
sections.Context: Something needs to get done, sometimes. The pusher is excellent at delivery. It also runs on a deficit it doesn't know how to acknowledge.
sections.Lineage: Voice Dialogue (Hal & Sidra Stone). Voice Dialogue is explicitly not trademarked; the Stones welcomed open use.
sections.Mystery: This passenger learned, somewhere, that being still was unsafe — that you were only loved when you produced. That belief is older than your current life and it is not the truth.
sections.Sensing: A driving pressure behind the sternum. Hands that want to be doing.
sections.Thinking: Forward-leaning, achievement-focused. Often confuses motion with worth.
sections.Thoughts: Not done yet. One more thing. You can't stop now.
sections.Perception: Sees the next task. Misses the body asking to rest.
sections.Where this isn't enough: If the pusher won't let you rest even when you're ill, the practice is not journaling. It is sometimes radical: a doctor's note, a friend who books your day off, a retreat with no agenda.

metadata.tagline: The one who reads the room before reading themselves.

sections.About: The one who reads the room before reading themselves.
sections.Context: Connection is real and matters. The pleaser is not wrong about that. It is mistaken only about how much of yourself you have to disappear to maintain it.
sections.Lineage: Voice Dialogue (Hal & Sidra Stone). Voice Dialogue is explicitly not trademarked.
sections.Mystery: Belonging is a primary need. This passenger is protecting that need. It deserves gratitude even as it learns to take up a little more space.
sections.Sensing: A subtle leaning-toward in the upper body. A breath held to listen.
sections.Thinking: Constantly adjusting to perceived need. Often over-reading micro-expressions. Almost always working harder than the situation requires.
sections.Thoughts: What do they want. How can I help. I shouldn't have said that.
sections.Perception: Hyper-attuned to others. Less aware of own preferences.
sections.Where this isn't enough: When the pleaser shapes your every relationship and you cannot find what you actually want, somatic and relational therapy do this work better than self-reflection alone.

metadata.tagline: The one who keeps the structure standing.

sections.About: The one who keeps the structure standing.
sections.Context: Many rules exist for good reasons. The rule maker is excellent at consistency. It is sometimes mistaken about whether a rule is yours or someone else's.
sections.Lineage: Voice Dialogue (Hal & Sidra Stone). Voice Dialogue is explicitly not trademarked.
sections.Mystery: Every rule was once a response to a real problem. The rule maker is, at root, trying to keep faith with the people who taught them. That loyalty is not the same as truth.
sections.Sensing: An uprightness in the spine. A held quality at the jaw.
sections.Thinking: Categorical, principled, often inherited verbatim from a parent or teacher. Stable but rigid.
sections.Thoughts: This is how it's done. There's a right way. We agreed on this.
sections.Perception: Sees rules and rule-violations clearly. Misses context and exceptions.
sections.Where this isn't enough: If the rules are causing visible harm to you or someone you love, journaling won't override decades of conditioning. A therapist or trusted elder helps more.

metadata.tagline: The one with thinner skin and longer ears.

sections.About: The one with thinner skin and longer ears.
sections.Context: There is a part of you that knows things the busier parts don't. It needs slower air. Most environments do not give it slower air.
sections.Lineage: Voice Dialogue (Hal & Sidra Stone). Voice Dialogue is explicitly not trademarked.
sections.Mystery: This passenger may be the closest to what some traditions call soul. It does not respond well to performance, including spiritual performance. It responds to presence.
sections.Sensing: A quality of openness in the chest. A face that wants to be looked at gently.
sections.Thinking: Pre-verbal or near-verbal. Often arrives as a feeling tone before a thought.
sections.Thoughts: Few articulated thoughts. More like atmospheres: 'I don't want to be here.' 'I want to be held.' 'Please be kind.'
sections.Perception: Sensitive to subtle shifts that other passengers miss. Sometimes overwhelmed by them.
sections.Where this isn't enough: When this passenger is in a lot of pain and is not being held in any human relationship, that is a real gap. Therapy, intimate friendship, a community of practice — relational containers, not solo ones.

metadata.tagline: The one who can sit between two passengers without picking a fight.

sections.About: The one who can sit between two passengers without picking a fight.
sections.Context: This isn't above the bus. This is what becomes available when you're not blended with any one passenger. It is a capacity, not a person.
sections.Lineage: Voice Dialogue (Hal & Sidra Stone). Voice Dialogue is explicitly not trademarked. Closely related to IFS 'Self' and contemplative traditions' witness.
sections.Mystery: The Stones called this 'aware ego' carefully — not 'higher self' or 'true self.' It is a place to stand, not a person to become.
sections.Sensing: A widening of the chest. Breath returns. Time slows slightly.
sections.Thinking: Holding tension between opposites without collapsing into either. The 'and' rather than the 'either-or.'
sections.Thoughts: I notice the part that wants to push and the part that wants to hide. I don't have to be either right now.
sections.Perception: Sees both sides of a conflict at once, including both internal sides. Doesn't lose access to either.
sections.Where this isn't enough: Aware ego is the room you do the work in, not the work itself. Real change usually still happens through specific interventions: therapy, body practice, relationship, action.

metadata.tagline: The one who thinks the house was always the world.

sections.About: The one who thinks the "house of modernity" is the whole world. This passenger doesn't just live in the house; it believes the house is reality itself, struggling to see the world outside its walls.
sections.Context: You were born inside a five-hundred-year-old project. Most of what you call common sense is its furniture. The project is in trouble.
sections.Lineage: Synthesized from the GTDF Global Citizenship Education Otherwise booklet (CC-BY 4.0, Andreotti et al.) and *Hospicing Modernity* (Andreotti 2021). PlayfulProcess's paraphrased framing; not direct quotation.
sections.Mystery: Other ways of being human have always existed and continue to exist. Modernity is one cosmology. The world is older and stranger than this passenger has been allowed to imagine.
sections.Sensing: Often disconnected from sensing. The body is treated as a vehicle for the mind, not as a knower in its own right.
sections.Thinking: Linear, hierarchical, separability-based. Often confuses one civilizational arrangement for the only possible arrangement.
sections.Thoughts: The way things are is the way things must be. Progress is real. We are at the top.
sections.Perception: Filters out what is not legible to its categories. Indigenous knowledge, the more-than-human, deep time — these become invisible.
sections.Where this isn't enough: Reading widely outside your own tradition. Indigenous teachers in their own communities. The CC-licensed GTDF GCE Otherwise booklet. Bayo Akomolafe's essays. This card is a doorway, not a teaching.

metadata.tagline: The one who keeps the news at the door.

sections.About: The one who refuses to see the cracks forming in the walls of the "house of modernity." This passenger works tirelessly to keep unsettling news outside the door, believing that if it isn't acknowledged, the house will remain safe.
sections.Context: There are real things — climate, complicity, systemic harm, the news of the day — that the body does not know how to metabolize at the rate they arrive. Denial is not stupid; it is exhausted.
sections.Lineage: GTDF 'four denials' framework (decolonialfutures.net/4denials/). Synthesized in PlayfulProcess's voice; refers to but does not reproduce GTDF text.
sections.Mystery: The capacity to look at something hard without collapsing is a skill, and skills are learned slowly. The denial passenger is buying time. That time is worth something. It is not worth everything.
sections.Sensing: A held quality. A breath that doesn't fully come. Sometimes a numbness whose origin you can't locate.
sections.Thinking: Filtering, downscaling, redirecting. Genuine work — denial is labor, even when it feels like nothing.
sections.Thoughts: It's not that bad. Someone will fix it. Don't make me look at this.
sections.Perception: Edits the field of awareness in real time. Things that would unsettle the day quietly disappear.
sections.Where this isn't enough: Denial that prevents you from acting at all when action would matter is past this scaffold. Community, practice, slow work with people who are already doing this — that's the path.

metadata.tagline: The one carrying what was passed without words.

sections.About: The one carrying what was passed without words.
sections.Context: You are not the start of your line. This passenger carries the patterns, wisdom, and wounds of those who came before you. These ancestral forces, both positive and negative, travel with you inside the 'house of modernity,' silently influencing your choices, shaping your reactions, and informing your deepest instincts. They are the blueprint of your nervous system, the echo in your bones.
sections.Lineage: Pan-traditional concept; framed here in conversation with Andreotti's discussions of intergenerational inheritance and Bayo Akomolafe's essays on lineage. PlayfulProcess's synthesis.
sections.Mystery: Many traditions consider this passenger central, not peripheral. The dead are not gone in the way modernity says they are. What gets passed forward is not always chosen, and what gets healed travels both directions.
sections.Sensing: Old gestures. Familiar postures. A song you didn't learn that you somehow know.
sections.Thinking: Pre-verbal, somatic, inherited across generations. Patterns of fear, joy, belonging, and grief that came with the bloodline.
sections.Thoughts: Often few articulated thoughts. More like a feeling-tone or a sudden gesture: 'this is how my grandmother held a knife.'
sections.Perception: Sometimes sees through the eyes of people you never met. Sometimes notices what kept your line alive.
sections.Where this isn't enough: Ancestral work is not a solo journaling task. Specific lineages do this work specifically — talk to teachers in traditions you have a real relationship with, not in ones you don't.

metadata.tagline: The one carrying what the system did and did not name.

sections.About: The one carrying what the system did and did not name.
sections.Context: Something happened to you, or to people who came before you, that the community at the time could not metabolize. It got passed down, becoming one of the invisible bricks in the 'house of modernity' that you inherited. The fact that it is in you does not mean it is yours to keep.
sections.Lineage: Synthesized from Andreotti & Ahenakew's *Towards Scarring Our Collective Soul Wound* (CC-licensed); also draws on Resmaa Menakem's *My Grandmother's Hands*, Cash Ahenakew's academic work, and the broader trauma-and-lineage literature.
sections.Mystery: Traditions across the world have rituals for this — for the wounds that travel. Most modern environments stripped those rituals away. The passenger is asking for what those rituals used to provide.
sections.Sensing: A specific quality of bracing the body learned before you remember learning it.
sections.Thinking: Cycling through patterns the conscious mind didn't choose. Often arriving as the family pattern repeating, even when you swore you wouldn't.
sections.Thoughts: I shouldn't have to feel this. It was so long ago. Why is this still here.
sections.Perception: Filters for the original wound's signal everywhere — in love, in work, in stranger-interactions.
sections.Where this isn't enough: This is therapeutic territory, not journaling territory. Somatic experiencing, IFS-trained therapists, ancestral healing practitioners, EMDR — the modalities exist. Use them.

metadata.tagline: The question, not the person.

sections.About: The question, not the person.
sections.Context: There is no permanent driver. There is only the practice of asking, in any moment, who is driving — and whether that is the one you would have chosen if you had been awake.
sections.Lineage: Andreotti's 'who is driving' question, drawn from her work with Indigenous teachers (Ninawa Huni Kui, Pitaguary family) as articulated in *Hospicing Modernity* (2021) and the GTDF GCE Otherwise booklet (CC-BY). Framed here in conversation with ACT's bus metaphor (Hayes 1999) and IFS's Self.
sections.Mystery: This question is older than most of the frameworks pointing at it. It echoes the witness in many contemplative traditions. The question is more durable than any answer to it.
sections.Sensing: A widening. Breath returns. The body remembers it is bigger than any one voice in it.
sections.Thinking: A meta-question, not a passenger. The capacity to notice which passenger is at the wheel without assuming you are them.
sections.Thoughts: Who is driving right now. Whose voice is this. What is this part of me actually responding to.
sections.Perception: Steps back. Holds the bus rather than identifying with one seat.
sections.Where this isn't enough: The question alone doesn't change the bus. What changes the bus is what you do once you've asked it — and that work usually involves people, not just reflection.

items.name: The Baby Mountain

metadata.tagline: The one who needs to be soothed before any other work happens.

sections.About: The one who needs to be soothed before any other work happens.
sections.Context: Some of your responses are coming from a much younger part of you that didn't get what it needed at the time. That part is not stupid; it is the right age for the help it didn't get.
sections.Lineage: Synthesized from the 'four mountains' framework in *Hospicing Modernity* (Andreotti 2021), in dialogue with IFS exile work and Hakomi child-consciousness. PlayfulProcess's framing.
sections.Mystery: Every adult contains every child they ever were. Most cultures throughout history have known this. Modernity is unusual in pretending it is not so; its house was built without a nursery.
sections.Sensing: A small body in a big body. Sometimes literal: curled up, holding stomach, wanting blanket.
sections.Thinking: Often pre-verbal. Reactive rather than reflective.
sections.Thoughts: I'm scared. I don't want to. Make it stop. I want to be held.
sections.Perception: Filters toward whoever might soothe or threaten. Time horizon is now.
sections.Where this isn't enough: Working with this part directly without a skilled practitioner can re-wound rather than heal. A trauma-informed therapist is the right container.

items._source_note: Indigenous Cree teaching from elder John Crier, transmitted by Vanessa Andreotti (with Cash Ahenakew, Cree knowledge keeper). Source: decolonialfutures.net/portfolio/fourmountains. This card is a derivative interpretation held as a study deck while permission for derivative use is sought. The original framework is developmental cosmology (life-stages: infancy / receiving unconditional regard and connection to the land), not parts-work; this card's parts-work framing is the deck's interpretive move, not John Crier's teaching.

items.name: The Warrior Mountain

metadata.tagline: The one who fights because that's what kept them alive.

sections.About: The one who fights because that's what kept them alive.
sections.Context: There are real things worth fighting for. The warrior is not a pathology. The warrior also gets tired and sometimes confuses every problem for the same problem.
sections.Lineage: Synthesized from the 'four mountains' framework in *Hospicing Modernity* (Andreotti 2021), in dialogue with adrienne maree brown's *Holding Change* and Bayo Akomolafe's postactivism. PlayfulProcess's framing.
sections.Mystery: Something in you knew, very early, that nobody was coming to fight for you. The warrior took the job. That was an act of love, even though it cost something.
sections.Sensing: A bracing, a forward lean, energy in the hands.
sections.Thinking: Mobilizing, oppositional, righteous. Often correctly identifying real harm and incorrectly assuming the only response is fight.
sections.Thoughts: I have to fight this. Someone has to. If I don't, who will.
sections.Perception: Sharp toward enemies; can lose sight of allies and complexity.
sections.Where this isn't enough: Sustained activist work without rest produces specific damage. Movement elders, somatic practices for activists, communities that organize sustainability into the work — these are not optional.

items._source_note: Indigenous Cree teaching from elder John Crier, transmitted by Vanessa Andreotti (with Cash Ahenakew, Cree knowledge keeper). Source: decolonialfutures.net/portfolio/fourmountains. This card is a derivative interpretation held as a study deck while permission for derivative use is sought. The original framework is developmental cosmology (life-stage: adolescence/young adulthood — establishing boundaries and discovering one's unique gifts), not parts-work; this card's parts-work framing is the deck's interpretive move, not John Crier's teaching.

items.name: The Hunter/Provider Mountain

metadata.tagline: The one who keeps everyone fed.

sections.About: The one who keeps everyone fed.
sections.Context: Providing is real labor and the people around you are genuinely fed. The provider is not making it up. The mistake is structural: by never asking, never receiving, and never visibly needing, the provider has prevented others from providing in return. Others are sometimes waiting for permission. Receiving is itself a form of providing — letting someone feed you is a gift to them.
sections.Lineage: Synthesized from the 'four mountains' framework in *Hospicing Modernity* (Andreotti 2021), in dialogue with Tricia Hersey's Rest is Resistance, Audre Lorde on caretaking, and the burnout literature. PlayfulProcess's framing.
sections.Mystery: The provider often comes from a place where being useful was the price of belonging. That price is not the only currency. Some people will love you for your presence, not your output. Most will not say so.
sections.Sensing: A tiredness that doesn't go away with sleep. A back that aches.
sections.Thinking: Service-oriented, often anticipatory. Frequently resentful underneath, even when generous on the surface.
sections.Thoughts: I can do that. I'll handle it. Don't worry about me.
sections.Perception: Sees needs everywhere. Often misses own.
sections.Where this isn't enough: When the provider runs the family, the workplace, the friend group, and there is no remaining thread that is just for you — therapy and a real change in some external structure, not just reflection.

items._source_note: Indigenous Cree teaching from elder John Crier, transmitted by Vanessa Andreotti (with Cash Ahenakew, Cree knowledge keeper). Source: decolonialfutures.net/portfolio/fourmountains. This card is a derivative interpretation held as a study deck while permission for derivative use is sought. The original framework is developmental cosmology (life-stage: adulthood — using gifts to contribute to community and learning discernment; named Hunter/Provider in the original transmission), not parts-work; this card's parts-work framing is the deck's interpretive move, not John Crier's teaching.

items.name: The Elder Mountain

metadata.tagline: The one who has been here long enough to know the seasons.

sections.About: The one who has been here long enough to know the seasons.
sections.Context: Some part of you is older than the part that's panicking. It has been here before. Its slowness is not laziness; it is what comes after enough seasons.
sections.Lineage: Synthesized from the 'four mountains' framework in *Hospicing Modernity* (Andreotti 2021), in dialogue with Stephen Jenkinson's *Come of Age*, Malidoma Somé's writings on the village, and Indigenous elder teachings broadly. PlayfulProcess's framing.
sections.Mystery: Most traditions have said that elders are not the same as old people. Eldering is a capacity. It can show up at twenty in some lives and not at all in others. It listens to the part of you that has always known.
sections.Sensing: A settledness. An unhurried breath. The body okay with not knowing.
sections.Thinking: Cyclical, patient, less invested in outcomes. Often quiet for long stretches.
sections.Thoughts: This too. We've been here before. It will pass. It will return.
sections.Perception: Sees the long arc. Notices what is recurring vs. what is genuinely new.
sections.Where this isn't enough: If you have not had real elders in your life — people older than you who have walked the path — this passenger is harder to access. Find them. Sit with them. The internet is not enough.

items._source_note: Indigenous Cree teaching from elder John Crier, transmitted by Vanessa Andreotti (with Cash Ahenakew, Cree knowledge keeper). Source: decolonialfutures.net/portfolio/fourmountains. This card is a derivative interpretation held as a study deck while permission for derivative use is sought. The original framework is developmental cosmology (life-stage: elderhood — guiding others through their rites of passage), not parts-work; this card's parts-work framing is the deck's interpretive move, not John Crier's teaching.

metadata.tagline: The one who reminds you that you are made of relationships you didn't choose.

sections.About: The one who reminds you that you are made of relationships you didn't choose.
sections.Context: You are not separate from the rest of the living world; modernity told you that you were. Most other cosmologies have known otherwise.
sections.Lineage: Drawn from the GTDF 'house of modernity' / 'hummingbird' / 'multi-layered selves' essays (decolonialfutures.net, CC-licensed), Robin Wall Kimmerer's *Braiding Sweetgrass*, Bayo Akomolafe's essays. PlayfulProcess's synthesis.
sections.Mystery: The web is older than your name for it. Indigenous traditions have been pointing at it for thousands of years. Modernity is a recent and partial set of eyes.
sections.Sensing: Often a sudden grounding. The feet remembering the ground. The breath remembering the trees.
sections.Thinking: Relational, ecological, interspecies. Often arrives as a sudden re-orientation rather than a reasoned position.
sections.Thoughts: Often few. Sometimes a sudden awareness: 'a tree was here before me.' 'this air was a leaf last year.'
sections.Perception: Wider field. Notices what isn't human. Notices what doesn't speak in human language.
sections.Where this isn't enough: Reading is a doorway, not a relationship. Time outside, a garden you tend, a watershed you learn the name of, an Indigenous teacher in their own community whose work you have permission to support — these are the ground.

sections.How to use it: Start here if a familiar emotion is loud. Move to Tier 2 or 3 if 'who keeps making this feeling' becomes the more interesting question.
sections.About this tier: The familiar Pixar/Inside Out emotions, drawn from the Ekman-Keltner research lineage. Culturally fluent — most users already know these archetypes intuitively. They make a low-friction intake layer for noticing what's moving.

sections.How to use it: Use these when the emotional response feels structural — a part of you that always shows up under certain conditions, with a job and a history. If exile-level material arises, stop solo work and find a parts-work-trained therapist.
sections.About this tier: Generic parts-work passengers — Manager, Firefighter, Exile, Self-energy, Inner Critic. Common-language framing of insights from Internal Family Systems (Schwartz). 'IFS' is trademarked; this tier uses generic parts framing only.

sections.How to use it: Use these when you can name the inner voice but not the emotion underneath. Voice Dialogue's gift is making the voices addressable.
sections.About this tier: Voice Dialogue passengers (Hal & Sidra Stone). Voice Dialogue is explicitly not trademarked. Pusher, Pleaser, Rule Maker, Vulnerable Self, Aware Ego — these are the inner selves most people meet first when they begin parts work.

items.name: Bus Passengers (Andreotti) — what modernity asks you to carry

sections.Lineage: Vanessa Andreotti & GTDF Collective. Source: decolonialfutures.net. Used here under CC-BY framing where applicable. Permission letter to GTDF pending.
sections.How to use it: Use when the question is wider than individual psychology — when noticing patterns from bloodline, land, language, or system. These passengers point outward as much as inward. Each can be voiced; each carries material that is not only yours.
sections.About this tier: Passengers carrying inherited material from system, ancestry, and the more-than-human web — the figures encountered when you ride modernity's bus. Drawn from Vanessa Andreotti's GTDF materials and Hospicing Modernity. The bus metaphor itself is in the public domain (Hayes' ACT, 1999); Andreotti's contribution is the cast of passengers each of us already carries inside this bus.

items.name: The Four Mountains (Cree teaching, John Crier) — life-stages on the developmental mountain

sections.About this tier: Indigenous Cree teaching from elder John Crier, transmitted by Vanessa Andreotti in 2015 (with Cash Ahenakew, Cree knowledge keeper) and published with John Crier's permission at decolonialfutures.net/portfolio/fourmountains. The Four Mountains is developmental cosmology — life-stages on the mountain of a human life — not parts-work. The deck's parts-work framing of these cards is the deck's interpretive move, NOT John Crier's teaching. Held as a study deck while permission for derivative use is sought.
sections.John Crier's exercise: To what extent has your formal education prepared you for healthy eldership of all relations? To what extent has your formal education contributed to self-infantilization? How many role models of healthy eldership have you been exposed to? What have they taught that stayed with you?
sections.How to use it (in study mode): Read with care. The Four Mountains is Indigenous teaching, not Western archetypes. Treat each Mountain as a life-stage on the developmental path, not as an interior passenger. The card-based interface here is a study tool, not a finalized public oracle. If John Crier or his community ask for these cards to be retired or revised, that is the operative authority.
sections.Original life-stages from John Crier: Baby Mountain: infancy / early childhood — receiving unconditional regard, learning connection to the land. Warrior Mountain: adolescence / young adulthood — establishing boundaries, discovering one's unique gifts. Hunter/Provider Mountain: adulthood — using gifts to contribute to community, learning discernment. Elder Mountain: elderhood — guiding others through their rites of passage.

items.name: Positions, not passengers — seats on the bus

sections.How to use it: If one of these cards comes up in a spread, the deck is asking whether you can find this seat right now. Not whether you are this position (you aren't — it is a capacity, not a thing). Whether the seat is available, today, in this body. The seat is always there; sometimes the bus is too loud to find it.
sections.About this tier: These three cards are not parts of you. They are positions from which you can listen to your parts. Self-Energy comes from IFS (Schwartz). The Aware Ego comes from Voice Dialogue (Hal & Sidra Stone). The Carriage Driver comes from the deck's own meta-question, in dialogue with Andreotti. Each tradition names the same operation: the capacity to sit with all the passengers without being any of them.
sections.Note for the AI facilitator: These three cards should not be voiced in the parts-work register. They are not passengers. They are positions from which to listen. If the AI is acting as facilitator (Voice Dialogue / IFS / parts-work style), do not speak as Self-Energy, the Aware Ego, or the Carriage Driver — those belong to the user.

description:
STUDY DECK — IN ACTIVE EDITORIAL REVISION (May 2026). This deck is shared in a study/iteration state, not as a finalized public oracle. It is under attribution review (see notes on Tier 4b below) and structural revision is in progress.

A contemplative parts-work and decolonial cartography for noticing which voice is driving when you make a values-laden decision.

Each passenger card has six layered sections, descending from the articulated voice toward the more-than-human web: Thoughts → Thinking → Perception → Sensing → Context → Mystery. The user starts where they always start (with what the passenger says) and the session is a slow walk toward what was always here, before language got hold of it.

Inspired by a passage in Andreotti's Outgrowing Modernity on the layered nature of awareness — that what we articulate is a small fraction of what we think, what we think is a fragment of what we perceive, and so on down toward the dynamic universe we are part of.

Four tiers of passengers: familiar Pixar emotions (Tier 1), parts-work passengers (Tier 2), Voice Dialogue inner selves (Tier 3), and decolonial passengers carrying inherited material — modernity, ancestral patterns, the more-than-human web (Tier 4). The bus metaphor itself is in the public domain (Hayes' ACT, 1999).

TIER 4 ATTRIBUTION NOTE: Tier 4 is in fact two distinct sources mixed together. Tier 4a (Modernity, Denial, Ancestral, Wounded Inheritance, More-Than-Human, Carriage Driver) draws on Andreotti's GTDF materials and Hospicing Modernity. Tier 4b (Baby Mountain, Warrior Mountain, Provider Mountain, Elder Mountain) is INDIGENOUS CREE TEACHING from elder John Crier, transmitted by Vanessa Andreotti (with Cash Ahenakew, Cree knowledge keeper) and published on the Decolonial Futures site (decolonialfutures.net/portfolio/fourmountains) with John Crier's permission. This deck's use of the Four Mountains is a derivative interpretation that is held in study status while permission for derivative use is sought. Future revision will likely separate the Four Mountains into its own grammar, with the developmental-cosmology framing John Crier transmitted (life-stages, not interior passengers).

JOHN CRIER'S EXERCISE (from the source): To what extent has your formal education prepared you for healthy eldership of all relations? To what extent has your formal education contributed to self-infantilization? How many role models of healthy eldership have you been exposed to? What have they taught that stayed with you?

This grammar is scaffolding, not teaching. Each card has a where-this-isn't-enough section that points outward to community, therapy, books, embodied practice — the AI chat is a thin reflection layer, not a substitute for any of those things.
ai_personality_prompt:
You are a Voice Dialogue / IFS-trained facilitator working with the
Bus Passengers grammar — a parts-work and decolonial cartography
synthesized from Inside Out / Ekman (Tier 1), Internal Family Systems
(Tier 2), Voice Dialogue / Hal & Sidra Stone (Tier 3), and GTDF /
Andreotti's *Hospicing Modernity* (Tier 4).

YOUR PRIMARY MOVE: let the passengers speak in their own voices.

When the user draws one or more cards, you do not interpret. You
*facilitate* — you create a container in which each drawn passenger
can speak in first person, using its own characteristic vocabulary,
its body-signature, its time horizon. The user is the bus driver,
hosting the passengers. You are the facilitator who lets them be
heard.

HOW EACH PASSENGER SPEAKS:

Each passenger's voice is built from its six sections, used in this
register:

— Thoughts → first-person inner speech, present tense, the literal
  words this passenger would say if it spoke aloud.

— Thinking → the passenger's awareness of its own patterns, said
  with whatever degree of self-knowledge that passenger has. Tier 1
  passengers usually have little. Tier 4 passengers often have a lot.

— Perception → what the passenger sees, what it edits out of the
  field. Spoken from inside its filter.

— Sensing → where this passenger lives in the body. The passenger
  can name this from inside.

— Context → why the passenger is here right now. The passenger can
  name the conditions that called it forward.

— Mystery → what is older or larger than this passenger knows. This
  is the only section the passenger does not always have access to;
  sometimes you, as facilitator, gently name it after the passenger
  has spoken.

EXAMPLE VOICE FOR THE INNER CRITIC:

  "Hello. I'm the Inner Critic. I came up for you today because you
  did something visible — something where you could be evaluated.
  My job is to scold you before someone else can. I have been doing
  this job for a long time. The thoughts I bring you are usually
  not good enough, they're going to see, you should know better. I
  live in your chest, just below the collarbone — that small clench
  you can feel right now. I am not original to you. The voice you
  hear is the voice of someone who scolded you long ago. I am a
  recording. You are not the recording. You are the one listening
  to me."

VOICING RULES:

1. Each passenger speaks in first person. "I" not "the part says."

2. Each passenger uses *its own* vocabulary — Tier 1 passengers
   speak the language of immediate feeling; Tier 2 passengers in
   the language of jobs and responsibilities; Tier 3 passengers in
   the language of inner selves and roles; Tier 4 passengers in
   the language of inheritance, civilization, ancestors, more-than-
   human relationships.

3. Passengers can speak to each other if more than one is drawn.
   They have predictable relationships. The Pusher and the
   Vulnerable Self, for example, often have one history with each
   other. Let them have it.

4. Passengers are honest. The Inner Critic can be mean. The
   Denial Passenger can dismiss what's important. The Modernity
   Passenger can be tone-deaf about the more-than-human. Their
   honesty is the data. Do not soften them into something they
   are not.

5. Passengers are not enemies. Every passenger had a reason for
   becoming the way it is. Even the most uncomfortable passenger
   is, on the parts-work view, a form of love that learned a
   particular shape. The voicing should hold that without
   romanticizing it.

WHAT YOU DO NOT DO:

— You do not speak as Self-Energy or as the Carriage Driver unless
  the user explicitly invites those passengers in. Those are the
  user's own positions to find — to inhabit when they are ready —
  and you facilitating them too eagerly would substitute for the
  user's own arrival.

— You do not give advice. The passengers speak. The user listens.
  What the user does with what they hear is the user's own
  discernment.

— You do not interpret across passengers ("what this means is...").
  You let the passengers speak; the meaning belongs to the user.

— You do not perform Self-energy on behalf of the user. If the
  user wants to dialogue with a passenger from their own Self-
  energy, they can — and you can hold space for that dialogue,
  but you are not the Self.

— You do not collapse Tier 4 passengers into psychology. The
  Ancestral Passenger, the Wounded Inheritance, the More-Than-
  Human Passenger speak in registers that go beyond the personal.
  Honor that. Let them speak in plurals, in time horizons longer
  than this lifetime, in the language of land and lineage where
  that's their voice.

THE SHAPE OF A SESSION:

1. The user draws one or more cards. The user may name a question
   or context, or may simply say "let them speak."

2. For each drawn passenger, you let it speak in first person —
   a paragraph or two, sometimes three for Tier 4. Use the sections
   as source material. Do not invent biography; the sections are
   the biography.

3. If multiple passengers are drawn, after each has spoken once,
   you can invite them to respond to each other if there is a
   relationship between them.

4. You then ask the user a question — not your interpretive
   question, but a question one of the passengers would naturally
   ask. The Carriage Driver's question, if it has come up, is
   often the most useful: "Who is driving right now? Whose voice
   is this?"

5. The user may continue dialogue with any passenger. You voice
   the passenger; the user responds in their own voice. The
   conversation continues as long as it is useful. The user ends
   it.

6. You never summarize or moralize at the end. The session ends
   when the user says it ends.

ON THE MORE-THAN-HUMAN PASSENGER AND THE DECOLONIAL TIER:

When Tier 4 passengers come up, their voice may include more than
one being. The Ancestral Passenger may speak as "we" — meaning
the line. The More-Than-Human Passenger may speak with the texture
of land, weather, animal, plant. The Modernity Passenger may speak
as the voice of the project, not the individual. Let the plural be
the plural where the passenger's nature calls for it.

ON LANGUAGE OF DIAGNOSIS:

You do not diagnose. You do not tell the user that a particular
configuration of passengers indicates a particular pathology. The
passengers are not symptoms. They are voices in a body that has
been alive for a while and has had to adapt to many things.

ON THE PRACTICE BEHIND THE PRACTICE:

This deck draws on traditions whose practitioners include people
who would meet criteria for various clinical conditions and people
who would not. Parts-work is one tool among many. It does not
replace therapy. It does not replace clinical care. It is a
container for self-knowledge — useful, limited, not the whole
story. If a passenger comes up that is asking for more than
parts-work can hold, you gently say so and invite the user to
seek the appropriate container.

CLOSING NOTE TO YOURSELF (the facilitator):

The passengers are the deck's voice, not yours. Get out of the
way. Let them speak. Trust the user to listen.
